title: Immersion Plate Waves, Through-Transmission, and Method Comparison
narrationScript: We finish immersion testing with three ideas that tie the method together, plate waves, the two ways to arrange the sound, and the honest comparison of contact against immersion. Start with plate waves. In a thin sheet, sound does not behave like it does in a thick block. When the thickness of the material is close to a wavelength, the front and back surfaces guide the energy and the whole sheet vibrates as a unit. These guided waves are called Lamb waves, or plate waves, and they travel along the sheet rather than straight through it. Immersion is the natural way to make them, because you can dial in the incident angle so precisely. Here is the idea. Tilt the transducer in the water so the beam hits the sheet at an angle. As you increase the angle, the refracted wave inside the plate gets steeper, and at certain specific angles the sound couples into a plate wave mode that rings along the sheet for a long distance. A small change in the incident angle switches you from one mode to another, so the angle is a control you set carefully and reproducibly. Because the wave travels along the sheet, a single plate wave setup can sweep a large area of thin material quickly and reveal disbonds, laminations, and cracks that a straight beam would struggle with. Thin aircraft skins and bonded sheet are classic plate wave work. Now the two arrangements. The first is pulse echo, the one you already know. A single transducer sends the pulse and then listens for the echoes that come back, the front surface, the back wall, and anything in between. Pulse echo gives you depth information, because the time of each echo tells you how deep the reflector is, and it needs access to only one side of the part. The second is through transmission. Here you use two transducers, one on each side of the part, perfectly aligned. One transmits, the other receives. There is no echo to read. Instead you watch the strength of the beam that makes it through. A flaw in the path blocks or scatters sound, so the received signal drops. Through transmission is sensitive to a flaw anywhere in the thickness and it tolerates noisy, attenuating material well, but it has two real limits. It tells you nothing about depth, because there is no time of flight to a reflector, only a single transmitted pulse. And it demands access to both sides and very careful alignment of the two probes, which is why immersion, with its rigid manipulators, suits it so well. A squirter system with a nozzle on each side of a moving plate is classic immersion through transmission. Pitch catch, using two angled probes on the same side, is a near relative used for specific geometries. Finally, the comparison that a Level Two must be able to make out loud. Immersion wins on consistency, coverage, and speed for production. The coupling never varies, the beam angle is set mechanically and exactly, focused probes give superb resolution and signal to noise, and the whole scan can be automated and recorded as a permanent map of the part. The price is a tank or a squirter, treated water, fixturing, and a part that can get wet, so immersion lives mostly in the shop, not the field. Contact wins on portability and access. The instrument is a handheld box, you can climb a structure with it, you can reach an awkward weld, and you need only a smear of couplant and one side of the part. The price is variability. Coupling depends on the operator's hand, surface condition, and pressure, and you cover only as much as you can reach and scan by hand. So the choice is not which method is better in the abstract. It is which fits the job. A field weld on a pressure vessel in service calls for contact angle beam. A truckload of identical forgings, or thin bonded panels, or a high volume plate line calls for immersion. Match the method to the part, the location, the production rate, and the flaws you expect, and you will choose correctly every time.
imagePrompt: Two aligned immersion transducers face each other across a thin metal panel submerged in a tank for through-transmission testing, one transmitting and one receiving, with a second nearby setup showing an angled probe launching a plate wave along a thin sheet.

sections.heading: Plate (Lamb) Waves in Thin Material
sections.body:
In a **thin sheet** sound does not behave as it does in a thick block. When the material **thickness is comparable to a wavelength**, the front and back surfaces guide the energy and the entire sheet vibrates as a unit. These guided modes are called **Lamb waves** (or **plate waves**), and they travel **along** the sheet rather than straight through it.

Immersion is the natural way to generate them, because the incident angle can be set so precisely. Tilt the transducer in the water so the beam strikes the sheet at an angle; as the angle increases the refracted wave inside the plate steepens, and at **specific incident angles** the energy couples into a plate-wave mode that propagates along the sheet for a long distance. A small change in incident angle switches you from one mode to another, so the angle is a control you set carefully and reproducibly.

Because the wave runs along the sheet, a single plate-wave setup sweeps a large area of thin material quickly and reveals **disbonds, laminations, and cracks** that a straight beam handles poorly. Thin aircraft skins and bonded sheet are classic plate-wave work.

sections.caption: Tilting an immersion transducer to a specific incident angle couples energy into a Lamb (plate) wave that propagates along a thin sheet, sweeping a large area for disbonds and cracks.

sections.heading: Pulse-Echo: One Probe, Depth Information
sections.body:
**Pulse-echo** is the arrangement you already know, run here through a water path. A **single transducer** transmits the pulse and then listens for the returning echoes — front surface, back wall, and anything between. Two properties define it:

- It provides **depth information**: the **time of flight** of each echo tells you how deep the reflector lies, so you can locate a flaw through the thickness.
- It needs access to **only one side** of the part.

This makes pulse-echo the general-purpose immersion mode for **forgings, plate, and billet**, where you want both detection and location, and where you can reach only the outer surface. The same front-surface and back-wall echo pattern from the previous lessons applies, with flaw echoes appearing between them on the time base. Its limit is that a flaw oriented edge-on to the beam, or hidden in a strongly scattering microstructure, may reflect little energy back — the situation where the next arrangement earns its place.

sections.heading: Through-Transmission: Two Probes, No Depth
sections.body:
**Through-transmission** uses **two transducers, one on each side** of the part and carefully aligned. One **transmits**, the other **receives** — there is no echo to read. Instead you monitor the **strength of the beam that arrives** at the receiver. A flaw in the path blocks or scatters sound, so the received signal **drops**; a clean path passes a strong signal.

Its strengths and limits are both sharp:

- **Strength:** sensitive to a flaw **anywhere through the thickness**, and it tolerates **noisy, attenuating material** well because the signal makes only one trip.
- **Limit 1:** it gives **no depth information** — there is no reflector time-of-flight, only a single transmitted pulse, so you know a flaw exists in the path but not where.
- **Limit 2:** it needs access to **both sides** and **precise alignment** of the two probes, which is exactly why immersion — with rigid manipulators or twin squirter nozzles — suits it so well.

A squirter system with a nozzle on each side of a moving plate is classic immersion through-transmission. **Pitch-catch**, with two angled probes on the **same** side, is a near relative used for specific geometries.

sections.caption: Pulse-echo uses one transducer and reads echo time for depth; through-transmission uses two aligned transducers and reads the drop in transmitted signal, with no depth information.

sections.title: Pulse-echo locates; through-transmission only detects
sections.body: Reach for **pulse-echo** when you need the **depth** of a flaw and can access one side. Reach for **through-transmission** when you need maximum sensitivity to a flaw **anywhere in the thickness** of noisy or attenuating material and can access **both sides** with aligned probes — accepting that it tells you a flaw is present but **not where**.

sections.heading: Contact Versus Immersion: The Honest Comparison
sections.body:
A Level II must be able to compare the two methods plainly. **Immersion** wins on **consistency, coverage, and speed**: the coupling never varies, the beam angle is set mechanically and exactly, focused probes give superb resolution and signal-to-noise, and the scan can be **automated and recorded** as a permanent map (B-scan and C-scan imaging). The price is a tank or squirter, treated water, fixturing, and a part that can **get wet** — so immersion lives mostly in the **shop**.

**Contact** wins on **portability and access**: the instrument is a handheld box, you can climb a structure or reach an awkward weld, and you need only a smear of couplant and **one side** of the part. The price is **variability** — coupling depends on the operator's hand, surface condition, and pressure — and you cover only what you can reach and scan by hand.

The choice is not which is better in the abstract; it is **which fits the job** — the part, the location, the production rate, and the flaws you expect.

sections.caption: Contact versus immersion methods — selecting by the demands of the job.
rows: Water path; highly consistent and repeatable
Fixed by wedge; change wedge to change angle
Set by manipulator tilt; any angle on the same probe
Limited to hand reach; usually manual
Full automated raster; permanent C-scan / B-scan map
Shop-based; needs tank or squirter and water
Field welds, awkward access, one-off work
High-volume forgings, plate lines, thin bonded panels

sections.heading: Choosing the Method in Practice
sections.body:
Put the comparison to work with concrete calls. A **field weld on an in-service pressure vessel** — access from one side, up on scaffolding, no chance to submerge it — is **contact angle-beam** work. A **truckload of identical forgings**, a **high-volume rolled-plate line**, or **thin bonded aircraft panels** that demand repeatable coverage and a permanent record are **immersion** jobs.

Work through the deciding questions every time:

- **Can the part get wet and reach the shop, or must it be tested in place?**
- **One side accessible, or both?** (Both-side access opens through-transmission.)
- **Do I need depth, or just detection?** (Depth favors pulse-echo.)
- **One part or thousands?** (Volume and a required record favor immersion.)
- **Is the material coarse and noisy?** (Focused immersion or through-transmission help.)

Answer those, and the method — contact or immersion, pulse-echo or through-transmission, straight beam, angle beam, or plate wave — chooses itself for the part in front of you.

figures.title: Generating a plate (Lamb) wave by incident angle

interactive.title: Contact or Immersion? Sort the Job Factors
interactive.intro: Each card describes a feature, requirement, or job situation. Decide whether it points to a contact setup or an immersion setup, then check your reasoning.

items.label: Inspecting a field weld on an in-service pressure vessel from scaffolding
items.why: One-sided access in the field with no chance to submerge the part — the portable handheld method fits.

items.label: Couplant is a smear of gel applied by hand and varies with pressure
items.why: Hand-applied film whose consistency depends on the operator is characteristic of contact testing.

items.label: Scanning 2,000 identical forgings to exactly the same setup, with a recorded C-scan map
items.why: High-volume, repeatable, automated coverage with a permanent record is the strength of immersion.

items.label: Beam angle is set by tilting the transducer on a manipulator, any angle on the same probe
items.why: Setting incidence by manipulator tilt — rather than swapping a fixed wedge — is an immersion capability.

items.label: A water path of about one-quarter the steel thickness is part of the setup
items.why: A controlled water path and its minimum-distance rule exist only in immersion.

items.label: A focused probe and a squirter sweep thin bonded panels for disbonds with high signal-to-noise
items.why: Focused probes, squirters, and plate-wave coverage of thin panels are immersion techniques.

items.label: Climbing a structure with a handheld flaw detector to reach an awkward joint
items.why: Portability and access to difficult locations are the deciding advantages of contact testing.

items.label: Only one side of the part is reachable and the work is a single one-off inspection
items.why: One-sided, low-volume work in the field points to contact rather than tank-based immersion.

checkQuestions.question: Lamb (plate) waves are best generated in immersion by:
options: Maximizing the water path
Tilting the transducer to a specific incident angle so energy couples into a guided mode
Using the highest available frequency at normal incidence
Removing all couplant from the path
checkQuestions.explanation: Plate waves form when the beam strikes a thin sheet at specific incident angles; immersion lets you set that angle precisely, and small angle changes switch between modes.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best distinguishes pulse-echo from through-transmission?
options: Pulse-echo needs two transducers; through-transmission needs one
Pulse-echo provides depth information from echo timing; through-transmission detects a flaw anywhere in the path but gives no depth
Through-transmission requires access to only one side
Pulse-echo cannot detect internal flaws
checkQuestions.explanation: A single pulse-echo probe reads echo time-of-flight for depth from one side; two aligned through-transmission probes read the drop in transmitted signal with no depth information.

checkQuestions.question: Through-transmission is especially useful for which situation?
options: When you must measure the exact depth of a reflector
When only one side of the part is accessible
When the material is noisy and attenuating and you need sensitivity to a flaw anywhere through the thickness
When no scanning fixture is available
checkQuestions.explanation: Because the sound makes a single trip, through-transmission tolerates attenuating, noisy material and is sensitive to a flaw anywhere in the path, though it cannot report depth.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with contact testing, the principal advantage of immersion testing is:
options: Lower equipment cost and full portability
Highly consistent coupling with automated, recordable coverage
No need to control beam angle
It works on parts that cannot get wet
checkQuestions.explanation: Immersion's water coupling is repeatable, the beam angle is set mechanically, and the scan can be automated and recorded — its core advantages for production work.

checkQuestions.question: A single, one-off field weld on an in-service structure, accessible from one side only, is best inspected by:
options: Immersion through-transmission in a tank
Contact angle-beam testing
A liquid-filled wheel probe in a tank
Immersion pulse-echo with a focused probe
checkQuestions.explanation: Field location, one-sided access, and low volume favor the portable, one-sided contact angle-beam method; immersion requires a tank or squirter and a part that can get wet.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, UT Technique Course §1.2.7 (plate waves)
CP-105-2024 UT Technique Course §1.2.8 (pulse-echo and through-transmission)
CP-105-2024 UT Technique Course §1.3 (comparison of contact and immersion methods)
General practice: method selection per codes such as ASME Section V, Articles 4 and 5
